source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 7.4 File owners (pp. 112-113)

questions.q: Change only the OWNER of the file myfile to albert.
questions.why: chown <user> <file> changes just the owning user.

questions.q: Set the owner of myfile to albert AND the owner group to GroupA in one command.
questions.why: chown user:group sets both the owner and the group at once.

questions.q: Change only the GROUP of the file 'file' to GroupA, using chown (leave the owner unchanged).
questions.why: chown :group (a leading colon, no user) changes only the owning group.

questions.q: What group does 'chown albert: file' assign to the file?
questions.why: With a trailing colon and no group name, chown uses the specified user's primary group.

questions.q: Recursively change the owner and group of everything under /dir1 to albert and GroupA.
questions.why: -R applies the change recursively to the directory and all files beneath it.

questions.q: Change the owner group of the file 'file' to group1 using the dedicated group command.
questions.why: chgrp changes a file's group; it also takes -R for recursion and -v to show changes.

options: Sets /etc/passwd's owner and group to match those of /etc/group
questions.why: --reference=RFILE applies RFILE's owner and group to the target file, instead of naming them explicitly.

questions.q: By default, which group owns a newly created file?
options: The primary group of the user who created the file
No group until one is assigned
questions.why: Every file belongs to one user and one group; by default the creating user's primary group owns it.
